title: Tapestry hatching and colour blending
subtitle: Techniques for creating gradual colour transitions in tapestry weaving using hatching, blending, and eccentric weft
excerpt: A hard boundary between two colours in tapestry looks graphic and sharp. Hatching, which interleaves short weft passes of two colours in alternating picks, creates a soft gradient between them. This entry covers hatching, blending, and eccentric weft shaping, the three main tools for colour transition in tapestry.
sourceNotes: Tapestry hatching and blending techniques synthesised from general tapestry weaving practice. Hatching (hachure) is a standard colour-blending technique used across European and Andean tapestry traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: A tapestry colour-blending technique in which alternating weft passes of two colours interleave along a shared boundary, producing a soft gradient rather than a hard line.
A weft pass that does not run straight across the full width of the warp but curves, loops, or reverses within the shed, used to build up curved shapes and shaded areas.
Letting the weft form a series of small arcs across the shed before beating, giving the weft enough length to pack down between the warp ends without pulling the selvedge inward.

A vertical gap between two colour areas in tapestry, formed when adjacent weft colours each turn back on their own side of a shared warp end. Slits can be left open or sewn closed after the piece is cut from the loom.

content.text: The most common problem in tapestry colour work is a hard, stepped edge between two areas.

softens the boundary by interleaving short passes of two weft colours across a shared zone. The two colours do not interlace with each other; each one simply passes into the other colour's territory before turning back. From a distance the passes mix optically into a gradient.
builds on hatching by allowing the weft to curve within the shed, creating shaded, rounded shapes that a straight horizontal pass cannot produce.
Step 1: Establish the two colour areas
Weave several plain picks in each colour to establish the base areas. Where the colours meet, choose whether to use a

join (each colour turns back on the same warp end, leaving a gap) or interlocking (the two weft loops interlock around a shared warp end before turning back). For hatching, you will be moving both colours into a shared transition zone, so neither approach is needed at the boundary itself.

Define a transition zone 2-3 cm wide at the colour boundary. Pass colour A into the zone for 4-6 warp ends before turning back. Pass colour B into the same zone from the other side for 4-6 ends before turning back. On alternate picks, let colour A extend a little further into the zone than colour B, then reverse the proportion. This produces a soft interleaving fringe of both colours visible in the finished piece. Always
the weft before beating to keep the warp ends from being pulled together.
Step 3: Use eccentric weft for curved transitions

To create a curved colour boundary such as a rounded hill or the arc of a sky, use eccentric weft: instead of passing the weft in a straight horizontal line, curve the weft around the drawn shape before turning back. Pass the weft only as far as the curve of the shape, turn, and build up the area inside the curve with extra picks. The area outside the curve is left unwoven for now, to be filled with the other colour. Beat gently and at an angle to maintain the curve.
Step 4: Work back to a level baseline

After building up a hatched or eccentric zone, weave across the full width of the warp in both colours for 2-3 picks to return to a level baseline. This prevents the built-up areas from pulling the warp into an arc. Check the warp tension across the full width after each eccentric or heavily hatched section.

items.problem: The hatch zone looks stripey rather than blended
items.cause: The two colours are entering the zone in alternating rows rather than varying proportions
items.fix: Vary how far each colour extends into the zone on each pick. Use 3:1, 2:2, and 1:3 proportions across successive picks rather than always meeting at the midpoint.

items.problem: The warp pulls into a pronounced curve at the eccentric weft area
items.cause: Too many picks were built up in one area before returning to a flat baseline
items.fix: Level out every 3-5 picks by filling in the surrounding unwoven areas. Never let one area build more than 1 cm higher than the rest of the warp before evening out.

items.problem: A slit has formed between the two colour areas
items.cause: The weft turns were too consistent, always meeting at the same warp end
items.fix: In the hatching zone, vary which warp end each colour turns on by 2-3 ends from pick to pick. This prevents a straight vertical gap from forming.
